1:
"Now the men of Israel had sworn in Mizpeh, saying, There shall not any of us give his daughter unto Benjamin to wife." The witnesses note the oath sworn at Mizpeh not to give their daughters to Benjamin. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were convened at Mizpeh before the war, after hearing the Levite's account. The witnesses note that they sware not to give a daughter to Benjamin to wife. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sworn in Mizpeh — the assembly; the not give his daughter — to Benjamin; the in their indignation — a great oath; the be not rash — be not rash with thy mouth (Eccl. 5:2).

The witnesses agree the chapter opens with a rash oath that would entangle them — "There shall not any of us give his daughter unto Benjamin to wife": in the heat of their indignation against the outrage they bound themselves by a great oath, never weighing what grief it might bring; the hasty vow, like Jephthah's before, would soon prove a snare; it teaches the danger of rash oaths sworn in passion, which bind the conscience and breed perplexity, and that the people of God must be slow and considerate in their vows, "be not rash with thy mouth... to utter any thing before God" (Eccl. 5:2), the oath sworn at Mizpeh against Benjamin.

So the witnesses make the verse the rash oath: The witnesses note the sworn in Mizpeh: the assembly. The witnesses note the not give his daughter: to Benjamin. The witnesses note the in their indignation: a great oath. The witnesses note the be not rash: be not rash with thy mouth (Eccl. 5:2). The expositors observe the sworn in Mizpeh: the assembly. The expositors observe the not give his daughter: to Benjamin. The expositors observe the in their indignation: a great oath. The expositors observe the be not rash: be not rash with thy mouth (Eccl. 5:2). The believer is taught to mark the sworn in Mizpeh, the not give his daughter, the in their indignation, the be not rash, and to be slow to vow and to mourn the bitter fruit of sin. The verse thus records that Israel had sworn at Mizpeh not to give their daughters to Benjamin — a rash oath in passion that would entangle them, the danger of hasty vows binding the conscience, the people of God slow to vow, not rash with the mouth (Eccl. 5:2).
2:
"And the people came to the house of God, and abode there till even before God, and lifted up their voices, and wept sore;" The witnesses note the people coming to the house of God at Shiloh and weeping sore before him. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was to Shiloh, where the tabernacle and ark were, not Bethel. The witnesses note that they abode there till even and wept sore. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the came to the house of God — at Shiloh; the abode till even — before God; the wept sore — their grief; the weeping before him — they shall come with weeping (Jer. 31:9).

The witnesses agree the people turned to God in deep mourning — "the people came to the house of God... and lifted up their voices, and wept sore": before the LORD at Shiloh they poured out their grief over the near-loss of a tribe; the weeping before God shows a heart at last broken over the dreadful fruit of sin and war; it teaches that the people of God do well to bring their sorrows to him and to mourn before his face, the place of true lamentation being at his house, "they shall come with weeping, and with supplications will I lead them" (Jer. 31:9), the people weeping before God at Shiloh.

So the witnesses make the verse wept sore: The witnesses note the came to the house of God: at Shiloh. The witnesses note the abode till even: before God. The witnesses note the wept sore: their grief. The witnesses note the weeping before him: they shall come with weeping (Jer. 31:9). The expositors observe the came to the house of God: at Shiloh. The expositors observe the abode till even: before God. The expositors observe the wept sore: their grief. The expositors observe the weeping before him: they shall come with weeping (Jer. 31:9). The believer is taught to mark the came to the house of God, the abode till even, the wept sore, the weeping before him, and to be slow to vow and to mourn the bitter fruit of sin. The verse thus records that the people came to the house of God and wept sore — a heart broken over the bitter fruit of sin and war, the people of God bringing their sorrows to him, coming with weeping and supplications (Jer. 31:9).
3:
"And said, O LORD God of Israel, why is this come to pass in Israel, that there should be to day one tribe lacking in Israel?" The witnesses note the people asking the LORD why a tribe should be lacking in Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes they addressed Jehovah, the only living and true God, the covenant God of all the twelve tribes. The witnesses note that they asked, why is one tribe lacking in Israel? The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the O LORD God of Israel — their cry; the why is this come to pass — their perplexity; the one tribe lacking — the loss; the search our ways — let us search and try our ways (Lam. 3:40).

The witnesses agree the people cried out in perplexity at the loss of a tribe — "why is this come to pass in Israel, that there should be to day one tribe lacking?": they grieved that the twelve were now as eleven, one almost blotted out; their lament, though heartfelt, looked more to the calamity than to their own share in the sin that caused it; it teaches that the people of God should mourn the breaches among the brethren, yet search their own hearts for the cause, not only asking "why" of God but judging themselves, "let us search and try our ways, and turn again to the LORD" (Lam. 3:40), the people lamenting the lacking tribe.

So the witnesses make the verse one tribe lacking: The witnesses note the O LORD God of Israel: their cry. The witnesses note the why is this come to pass: their perplexity. The witnesses note the one tribe lacking: the loss. The witnesses note the search our ways: let us search and try our ways (Lam. 3:40). The expositors observe the O LORD God of Israel: their cry. The expositors observe the why is this come to pass: their perplexity. The expositors observe the one tribe lacking: the loss. The expositors observe the search our ways: let us search and try our ways (Lam. 3:40). The believer is taught to mark the O LORD God of Israel, the why is this come to pass, the one tribe lacking, the search our ways, and to be slow to vow and to mourn the bitter fruit of sin. The verse thus records that the people asked the LORD why a tribe should be lacking — grieving the breach among the brethren, yet to search their own hearts for the cause, not only asking why but judging themselves, searching and trying their ways (Lam. 3:40).
4:
"And it came to pass on the morrow, that the people rose early, and built there an altar, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings." The witnesses note the people rising early, building an altar, and offering burnt and peace offerings. The witnesses note that Gill notes the day after their fasting and prayer, a deep sense of their case being upon them, they rose early. The witnesses note that they built an altar and offered burnt and peace offerings. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rose early — on the morrow; the built an altar — to seek God; the burnt and peace offerings — atonement and communion; the peace by the blood — peace through the blood of his cross (Col. 1:20).

The witnesses agree the people sought God with sacrifice and worship — "the people rose early, and built there an altar, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings": in their distress they drew near to God by sacrifice, the burnt offering for atonement and the peace offering for communion; the seeking of God with offerings shows a people turning to him for mercy and reconciliation in their trouble; it teaches that the way to God in distress is through sacrifice, pointing on to the one offering by which we have peace, "having made peace through the blood of his cross" (Col. 1:20), the people offering burnt and peace offerings.

So the witnesses make the verse burnt and peace offerings: The witnesses note the rose early: on the morrow. The witnesses note the built an altar: to seek God. The witnesses note the burnt and peace offerings: atonement and communion. The witnesses note the peace by the blood: peace through the blood of his cross (Col. 1:20). The expositors observe the rose early: on the morrow. The expositors observe the built an altar: to seek God. The expositors observe the burnt and peace offerings: atonement and communion. The expositors observe the peace by the blood: peace through the blood of his cross (Col. 1:20). The believer is taught to mark the rose early, the built an altar, the burnt and peace offerings, the peace by the blood, and to be slow to vow and to mourn the bitter fruit of sin. The verse thus records that the people built an altar and offered burnt and peace offerings — seeking God for mercy and reconciliation in their trouble, the way to him through sacrifice, pointing to the one offering by which we have peace through the blood of the cross (Col. 1:20).
5:
"And the children of Israel said, Who is there among all the tribes of Israel that came not up with the congregation unto the LORD? For they had made a great oath..." The witnesses note Israel asking who had not come up to Mizpeh, bound by a great oath. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was asked to bring to justice those who came not up, by their oath. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the verse touches the great oath they had made. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the who came not up — to the LORD; the a great oath — they had made; the whoever came not — should die; the the snare of vows — after vows to make enquiry (Prov. 20:25).

The witnesses agree the people, mindful of another oath, sought who had failed to come up — "Who is there... that came not up... unto the LORD? for they had made a great oath": they had bound themselves that whoever came not to the assembly should die, and now inquired who had broken it; the second great oath, like the first, would lead them into a hard and bloody course; it teaches again the entangling power of rash vows, which drive men from one strait to another, and that the people of God must weigh the consequence before binding themselves, "it is a snare to the man who devoureth that which is holy, and after vows to make enquiry" (Prov. 20:25), Israel asking who came not up to Mizpeh.

So the witnesses make the verse a great oath: The witnesses note the who came not up: to the LORD. The witnesses note the a great oath: they had made. The witnesses note the whoever came not: should die. The witnesses note the the snare of vows: after vows to make enquiry (Prov. 20:25). The expositors observe the who came not up: to the LORD. The expositors observe the a great oath: they had made. The expositors observe the whoever came not: should die. The expositors observe the the snare of vows: after vows to make enquiry (Prov. 20:25). The believer is taught to mark the who came not up, the a great oath, the whoever came not, the the snare of vows, and to be slow to vow and to mourn the bitter fruit of sin. The verse thus records that Israel asked who had not come up, bound by a great oath — the entangling power of rash vows driving men from one strait to another, the people of God to weigh the consequence, the snare of vowing rashly and after enquiring (Prov. 20:25).
6:
"And the children of Israel repented them for Benjamin their brother, and said, There is one tribe cut off from Israel this day." The witnesses note Israel grieving and relenting toward Benjamin their brother. The witnesses note that Gill notes not that the war was unjust, but they grieved for the severity, especially toward the innocent. The witnesses note that they said, one tribe is cut off from Israel this day. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the repented for Benjamin — their brother; the one tribe cut off — from Israel; the grief after justice — brotherly love; the remember mercy — restore in the spirit of meekness (Gal. 6:1).

The witnesses agree the people's anger gave way to brotherly grief — "the children of Israel repented them for Benjamin their brother... one tribe cut off from Israel": they sorrowed that, in punishing the guilty, a whole tribe had been all but destroyed, brother turned against brother; the relenting toward Benjamin shows that the love of brethren reasserted itself once justice was done; it teaches that the people of God, even in righteous severity, are to remember mercy and to grieve at the wounds of the household of faith, "if a man be overtaken in a fault... restore such an one in the spirit of meekness" (Gal. 6:1), Israel repenting for Benjamin their brother.

So the witnesses make the verse repented for Benjamin: The witnesses note the repented for Benjamin: their brother. The witnesses note the one tribe cut off: from Israel. The witnesses note the grief after justice: brotherly love. The witnesses note the remember mercy: restore in the spirit of meekness (Gal. 6:1). The expositors observe the repented for Benjamin: their brother. The expositors observe the one tribe cut off: from Israel. The expositors observe the grief after justice: brotherly love. The expositors observe the remember mercy: restore in the spirit of meekness (Gal. 6:1). The believer is taught to mark the repented for Benjamin, the one tribe cut off, the grief after justice, the remember mercy, and to be slow to vow and to mourn the bitter fruit of sin. The verse thus records that Israel grieved and relented toward Benjamin their brother — the love of brethren reasserting itself once justice was done, the people of God remembering mercy even in righteous severity, restoring in the spirit of meekness (Gal. 6:1).
7:
"How shall we do for wives for them that remain, seeing we have sworn by the LORD that we will not give them of our daughters to wives?" The witnesses note Israel's quandary how to find wives for the survivors without breaking their oath. The witnesses note that Gill notes it seems they knew of the six hundred hid in the rock Rimmon. The witnesses note that Clarke sets the perplexity, how shall we do for wives? The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the how shall we do — for wives; the them that remain — the survivors; the we have sworn — the oath; the keep the mouth — keepeth his mouth keepeth his soul from troubles (Prov. 21:23).

The witnesses agree the oath had brought them into a hard strait — "How shall we do for wives for them that remain, seeing we have sworn... not to give them of our daughters?": they would preserve the tribe, yet were bound by their vow not to give their own daughters; the perplexity born of their rash oath shows how a hasty vow ties men in knots of their own making; it teaches that the entanglements of rash promises are the fruit of not weighing our words, and that men, having bound themselves unwisely, are driven to shifts and devices to escape, "whoso keepeth his mouth and his tongue keepeth his soul from troubles" (Prov. 21:23), Israel's quandary over wives for Benjamin.

So the witnesses make the verse how shall we do: The witnesses note the how shall we do: for wives. The witnesses note the them that remain: the survivors. The witnesses note the we have sworn: the oath. The witnesses note the keep the mouth: keepeth his mouth keepeth his soul from troubles (Prov. 21:23). The expositors observe the how shall we do: for wives. The expositors observe the them that remain: the survivors. The expositors observe the we have sworn: the oath. The expositors observe the keep the mouth: keepeth his mouth keepeth his soul from troubles (Prov. 21:23). The believer is taught to mark the how shall we do, the them that remain, the we have sworn, the keep the mouth, and to be slow to vow and to mourn the bitter fruit of sin. The verse thus records that Israel was perplexed how to find wives without breaking their oath — the knots a hasty vow ties, the entanglements of rash promises, men driven to shifts to escape what they bound unwisely, keeping the mouth keeping the soul from troubles (Prov. 21:23).
8:
"And they said, What one is there of the tribes of Israel that came not up to Mizpeh to the LORD? And, behold, there came none to the camp from Jabeshgilead to the assembly." The witnesses note that Jabesh-gilead had sent none to the assembly at Mizpeh. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was asked to find a way for wives as well as to do justice. The witnesses note that none came to the camp from Jabesh-gilead. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the what one came not up — to Mizpeh; the Jabesh-gilead — none came; the under the oath — and a source of wives; the forsaking the assembly — forsake not the assembling (Heb. 10:25).

The witnesses agree the search found Jabesh-gilead had stayed away — "there came none to the camp from Jabeshgilead to the assembly": that city alone had failed to join the congregation, and so fell under the oath; the seizing on their absence both to fulfil the oath and to supply wives shows the mingled motives of justice and expediency; it teaches that men in their schemes often join a show of righteous judgment to their own ends, and warns that the keeping of one oath was made the occasion of a fearful deed, "they that forsake the assembling of themselves together" come under reproof (cf. Heb. 10:25), Jabesh-gilead found absent from Mizpeh.

So the witnesses make the verse none from Jabesh-gilead: The witnesses note the what one came not up: to Mizpeh. The witnesses note the Jabesh-gilead: none came. The witnesses note the under the oath: and a source of wives. The witnesses note the forsaking the assembly: forsake not the assembling (Heb. 10:25). The expositors observe the what one came not up: to Mizpeh. The expositors observe the Jabesh-gilead: none came. The expositors observe the under the oath: and a source of wives. The expositors observe the forsaking the assembly: forsake not the assembling (Heb. 10:25). The believer is taught to mark the what one came not up, the Jabesh-gilead, the under the oath, the forsaking the assembly, and to be slow to vow and to mourn the bitter fruit of sin. The verse thus records that Jabesh-gilead had sent none to the assembly — the mingled motives of justice and expediency seizing on their absence, men joining a show of righteous judgment to their own ends, forsaking not the assembling reproved (Heb. 10:25).
9:
"For the people were numbered, and, behold, there were none of the inhabitants of Jabeshgilead there." The witnesses note the numbering confirming that none of Jabesh-gilead were present. The witnesses note that Gill notes they numbered to know who came and who did not, and whether Jabesh-gilead were there. The witnesses note that none of the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead were found. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the people were numbered — the muster; the none of Jabesh-gilead — found wanting; the resolve on the oath — confirmed; the obedience over form — to obey is better than sacrifice (1 Sam. 15:22).

The witnesses agree the muster confirmed the absence of Jabesh-gilead — "the people were numbered, and... there were none of the inhabitants of Jabeshgilead there": on counting the assembly, that city's men were found wanting; the careful numbering shows the people's resolve to act on their oath, yet the use to which it was put was grievous; it teaches that exactness in keeping an oath is no virtue when the oath itself or its execution leads to evil, and that the people of God must beware lest a scrupulous keeping of their word become the door to wrong, "to obey is better than sacrifice" (1 Sam. 15:22), the numbering confirming Jabesh-gilead's absence.

So the witnesses make the verse none of Jabesh-gilead: The witnesses note the the people were numbered: the muster. The witnesses note the none of Jabesh-gilead: found wanting. The witnesses note the resolve on the oath: confirmed. The witnesses note the obedience over form: to obey is better than sacrifice (1 Sam. 15:22). The expositors observe the the people were numbered: the muster. The expositors observe the none of Jabesh-gilead: found wanting. The expositors observe the resolve on the oath: confirmed. The expositors observe the obedience over form: to obey is better than sacrifice (1 Sam. 15:22). The believer is taught to mark the the people were numbered, the none of Jabesh-gilead, the resolve on the oath, the obedience over form, and to be slow to vow and to mourn the bitter fruit of sin. The verse thus records that the numbering confirmed Jabesh-gilead's absence — exactness in an oath no virtue when its execution leads to evil, the people of God beware lest scrupulous word-keeping become a door to wrong, to obey being better than sacrifice (1 Sam. 15:22).
10:
"And the congregation sent thither twelve thousand men of the valiantest, and commanded them, saying, Go and smite the inhabitants of Jabeshgilead with the edge of the sword..." The witnesses note the congregation sending twelve thousand to smite Jabesh-gilead. The witnesses note that Gill notes they sent twelve thousand of the valiantest of their army. The witnesses note that they commanded them to smite the inhabitants with the sword. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the twelve thousand — the valiantest; the go and smite — jabesh-gilead; the with the sword — the severity; the evil to evil — they proceed from evil to evil (Jer. 9:3).

The witnesses agree the people resorted to a fearful severity to fulfil their oath — "Go and smite the inhabitants of Jabeshgilead with the edge of the sword": to make good their vow against those who came not to Mizpeh, they sent a host to destroy a whole city; the turning of the oath into the occasion of more bloodshed shows how one rash bond led to further grievous deeds; it teaches that the entanglement of an unwise vow may drive men into compounded evil, and warns the people of God against the course that adds wrong to wrong in the name of keeping faith, "they proceed from evil to evil, and they know not me, saith the LORD" (Jer. 9:3), the host sent to smite Jabesh-gilead.

So the witnesses make the verse smite Jabesh-gilead: The witnesses note the twelve thousand: the valiantest. The witnesses note the go and smite: Jabesh-gilead. The witnesses note the with the sword: the severity. The witnesses note the evil to evil: they proceed from evil to evil (Jer. 9:3). The expositors observe the twelve thousand: the valiantest. The expositors observe the go and smite: Jabesh-gilead. The expositors observe the with the sword: the severity. The expositors observe the evil to evil: they proceed from evil to evil (Jer. 9:3). The believer is taught to mark the twelve thousand, the go and smite, the with the sword, the evil to evil, and to wonder at the mercy that preserves a tribe. The verse thus records that the congregation sent twelve thousand to smite Jabesh-gilead — the oath turned into the occasion of more bloodshed, one rash bond leading to compounded evil, men proceeding from evil to evil (Jer. 9:3).
11:
"And this is the thing that ye shall do, Ye shall utterly destroy every male, and every woman that hath lain by man." The witnesses note the charge to destroy the men and the married women of Jabesh-gilead. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was the thing given them in charge to execute. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the command to utterly destroy every male and every woman that had known man. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the this is the thing — the charge; the utterly destroy every male — the sentence; the every woman that hath lain by man — the severity; the count the cost — better not to vow than vow and not pay (Eccl. 5:5).

The witnesses agree the sentence was sweeping and severe — "Ye shall utterly destroy every male, and every woman that hath lain by man": the whole city, save the unmarried maidens, was given to the sword; the dreadful execution of their oath shows the harsh extremity to which their self-made bond carried them; it teaches the grievous cost of vows rashly sworn, which exact a price in blood and sorrow far beyond what was foreseen, and bids the people of God count well the cost before they bind themselves, "better is it that thou shouldest not vow, than that thou shouldest vow and not pay" (Eccl. 5:5) — yet here the paying was itself a sorrow, the charge against Jabesh-gilead.

So the witnesses make the verse utterly destroy: The witnesses note the this is the thing: the charge. The witnesses note the utterly destroy every male: the sentence. The witnesses note the every woman that hath lain by man: the severity. The witnesses note the count the cost: better not to vow than vow and not pay (Eccl. 5:5). The expositors observe the this is the thing: the charge. The expositors observe the utterly destroy every male: the sentence. The expositors observe the every woman that hath lain by man: the severity. The expositors observe the count the cost: better not to vow than vow and not pay (Eccl. 5:5). The believer is taught to mark the this is the thing, the utterly destroy every male, the every woman that hath lain by man, the count the cost, and to wonder at the mercy that preserves a tribe. The verse thus records that the charge was to destroy the men and married women of Jabesh-gilead — the harsh extremity to which their self-made bond carried them, the grievous cost of rash vows, better not to vow than to vow (Eccl. 5:5).
12:
"And they found among the inhabitants of Jabeshgilead four hundred young virgins... and they brought them unto the camp to Shiloh, which is in the land of Canaan." The witnesses note four hundred young virgins found and brought to the camp at Shiloh. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were damsels that were virgins, judged by their age. The witnesses note that they brought them to the camp to Shiloh in Canaan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the found among them — four hundred; the young virgins — spared; the brought to Shiloh — the camp; the mercy in wrath — in wrath remember mercy (Hab. 3:2).

The witnesses agree four hundred maidens were spared and brought to Shiloh — "they found... four hundred young virgins... and brought them unto the camp to Shiloh": these alone were kept alive, to be given as wives to the survivors of Benjamin; the sparing of the maidens, in the midst of so much death, was meant to repair the breach in the tribe; it teaches that even amid severe judgment a way was opened for the preservation of Benjamin, and that God's providence wrought to keep the tribe from perishing utterly, "in wrath remember mercy" (Hab. 3:2), the four hundred virgins brought to Shiloh.

So the witnesses make the verse four hundred virgins: The witnesses note the found among them: four hundred. The witnesses note the young virgins: spared. The witnesses note the brought to Shiloh: the camp. The witnesses note the mercy in wrath: in wrath remember mercy (Hab. 3:2). The expositors observe the found among them: four hundred. The expositors observe the young virgins: spared. The expositors observe the brought to Shiloh: the camp. The expositors observe the mercy in wrath: in wrath remember mercy (Hab. 3:2). The believer is taught to mark the found among them, the young virgins, the brought to Shiloh, the mercy in wrath, and to wonder at the mercy that preserves a tribe. The verse thus records that four hundred virgins were spared and brought to Shiloh — a way opened to repair the breach in Benjamin, God's providence keeping the tribe from perishing, in wrath remembering mercy (Hab. 3:2).
13:
"And the whole congregation sent some to speak to the children of Benjamin that were in the rock Rimmon, and to call peaceably unto them." The witnesses note the congregation sending to proclaim peace to the Benjamites at the rock Rimmon. The witnesses note that Gill notes they sent messengers to call them and desire them to come. The witnesses note that Clarke notes they called peaceably unto them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sent some to speak — to Benjamin; the in the rock Rimmon — the remnant; the call peaceably — reconciliation; the the work of peace — the work of righteousness shall be peace (Isa. 32:17).

The witnesses agree the congregation now sought reconciliation with the remnant of Benjamin — "sent some to speak to the children of Benjamin... and to call peaceably unto them": to the six hundred who had fled to the rock Rimmon they proclaimed peace, that the breach might be healed; the offer of peace to the estranged brethren shows the way of reconciliation after just chastisement; it teaches that the people of God are to seek peace and the restoring of the fallen, proclaiming reconciliation to those once at enmity, "the work of righteousness shall be peace" (Isa. 32:17), the congregation calling peaceably to Benjamin.

So the witnesses make the verse call peaceably: The witnesses note the sent some to speak: to Benjamin. The witnesses note the in the rock Rimmon: the remnant. The witnesses note the call peaceably: reconciliation. The witnesses note the the work of peace: the work of righteousness shall be peace (Isa. 32:17). The expositors observe the sent some to speak: to Benjamin. The expositors observe the in the rock Rimmon: the remnant. The expositors observe the call peaceably: reconciliation. The expositors observe the the work of peace: the work of righteousness shall be peace (Isa. 32:17). The believer is taught to mark the sent some to speak, the in the rock Rimmon, the call peaceably, the the work of peace, and to wonder at the mercy that preserves a tribe. The verse thus records that the congregation proclaimed peace to Benjamin at the rock Rimmon — the way of reconciliation after just chastisement, the people of God restoring the fallen and proclaiming peace, the work of righteousness being peace (Isa. 32:17).
14:
"And Benjamin came again at that time; and they gave them wives which they had saved alive of the women of Jabeshgilead: and yet so they sufficed them not." The witnesses note Benjamin returning and receiving the women of Jabesh-gilead, who yet were not enough. The witnesses note that Gill notes the six hundred returned, trusting the assurances of peace. The witnesses note that the women sufficed them not, four hundred for six hundred. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Benjamin came again — the remnant returned; the gave them wives — of Jabesh-gilead; the sufficed them not — four hundred for six; the the way is hard — the way of transgressors is hard (Prov. 13:15).

The witnesses agree the remnant returned and were given wives, yet not enough — "Benjamin came again... they gave them wives... and yet so they sufficed them not": the four hundred maidens fell short of the six hundred men; the partial supply left two hundred still without wives, and the matter unfinished; it teaches that the remedies of man's devising are often incomplete, leaving a lack that drives to further shifts, and that the tangles of a rash course are not easily undone, "the way of transgressors is hard" (Prov. 13:15), the women of Jabesh-gilead not sufficing Benjamin.

So the witnesses make the verse sufficed them not: The witnesses note the Benjamin came again: the remnant returned. The witnesses note the gave them wives: of Jabesh-gilead. The witnesses note the sufficed them not: four hundred for six. The witnesses note the the way is hard: the way of transgressors is hard (Prov. 13:15). The expositors observe the Benjamin came again: the remnant returned. The expositors observe the gave them wives: of Jabesh-gilead. The expositors observe the sufficed them not: four hundred for six. The expositors observe the the way is hard: the way of transgressors is hard (Prov. 13:15). The believer is taught to mark the Benjamin came again, the gave them wives, the sufficed them not, the the way is hard, and to wonder at the mercy that preserves a tribe. The verse thus records that Benjamin returned and received wives who yet sufficed not — the remedies of man's devising incomplete, the tangles of a rash course not easily undone, the way of transgressors hard (Prov. 13:15).
15:
"And the people repented them for Benjamin, because that the LORD had made a breach in the tribes of Israel." The witnesses note the people grieving that the LORD had made a breach in the tribes. The witnesses note that Gill notes they repented they had destroyed all the women and saved no more of Jabesh-gilead. The witnesses note that the LORD had made a breach in the tribes of Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the repented for Benjamin — they grieved; the the LORD had made a breach — his hand; the in the tribes — his chastisement; the bear his indignation — i will bear the indignation of the LORD (Mic. 7:9).

The witnesses agree the people owned the LORD's hand in the breach — "the people repented them for Benjamin, because that the LORD had made a breach in the tribes of Israel": they grieved, and saw that it was God who had broken in upon them in judgment for the sin of the nation; the acknowledging of the LORD's hand in the calamity shows a measure of right discernment, that the breach was his chastisement; it teaches that the troubles of God's people are from his hand for their sin, and are to be received as his correction, that they may humble themselves and seek his mercy, "I will bear the indignation of the LORD, because I have sinned against him" (Mic. 7:9), the LORD having made a breach in the tribes.

So the witnesses make the verse a breach in the tribes: The witnesses note the repented for Benjamin: they grieved. The witnesses note the the LORD had made a breach: his hand. The witnesses note the in the tribes: his chastisement. The witnesses note the bear his indignation: I will bear the indignation of the LORD (Mic. 7:9). The expositors observe the bear his indignation: I will bear the indignation of the LORD (Mic. 7:9). The verse thus records that the people grieved that the LORD had made a breach in the tribes — owning his hand in the chastisement for the nation's sin, the troubles of God's people his correction to humble them, bearing the indignation of the LORD because they had sinned (Mic. 7:9).
16:
"Then the elders of the congregation said, How shall we do for wives for them that remain, seeing the women are destroyed out of Benjamin?" The witnesses note the elders again perplexed how to provide wives for the rest of Benjamin. The witnesses note that Gill notes there were not wives enough, so they consulted again for another expedient. The witnesses note that they asked, how shall we do for wives, the women being destroyed out of Benjamin? The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the elders said — again; the how shall we do — for wives; the the women destroyed — out of Benjamin; the the train of trouble — fools make a mock at sin (Prov. 14:9).

The witnesses agree the elders were driven back to their perplexity — "How shall we do for wives for them that remain, seeing the women are destroyed out of Benjamin?": the lack still remained, and they sought yet another device; the repeated quandary shows how the consequences of the war and the oath kept entangling them; it teaches that the troubles bred by sin and rash vows are not soon ended, but multiply and press for further remedy, and that man's wisdom is taxed to undo the knots of his own folly, "fools make a mock at sin" yet bear its long train of trouble (cf. Prov. 14:9), the elders perplexed again over wives for Benjamin.

So the witnesses make the verse how shall we do: The witnesses note the the elders said: again. The witnesses note the how shall we do: for wives. The witnesses note the the women destroyed: out of Benjamin. The witnesses note the the train of trouble: fools make a mock at sin (Prov. 14:9). The expositors observe the the elders said: again. The expositors observe the how shall we do: for wives. The expositors observe the the women destroyed: out of Benjamin. The verse thus records that the elders were again perplexed how to provide wives — the consequences of war and oath keeping them entangled, troubles bred by sin not soon ended but multiplying, man's wisdom taxed to undo his own folly (Prov. 14:9).
17:
"And they said, There must be an inheritance for them that be escaped of Benjamin, that a tribe be not destroyed out of Israel." The witnesses note the resolve that Benjamin's inheritance be preserved and the tribe not destroyed. The witnesses note that Gill notes the escaped were the six hundred; four hundred had wives, two hundred wanted. The witnesses note that a tribe be not destroyed out of Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the an inheritance — for the escaped; the them that be escaped — of Benjamin; the a tribe not destroyed — out of Israel; the God preserves his people — the LORD will not forsake his people (1 Sam. 12:22).

The witnesses agree the people were resolved to preserve Benjamin's place among the tribes — "There must be an inheritance for them that be escaped... that a tribe be not destroyed out of Israel": they would not let one of the twelve be blotted out, but sought to continue its inheritance; the determination to preserve the tribe shows the care for the integrity of the covenant people, that all twelve should remain; it teaches that God's purpose held to preserve every tribe of Israel, the providence that would not let Benjamin perish — from which tribe would come both Saul and the apostle Paul — fulfilling his faithfulness, "the LORD will not forsake his people for his great name's sake" (1 Sam. 12:22), the resolve that Benjamin be not destroyed.

So the witnesses make the verse a tribe not destroyed: The witnesses note the an inheritance: for the escaped. The witnesses note the them that be escaped: of Benjamin. The witnesses note the a tribe not destroyed: out of Israel. The witnesses note the God preserves his people: the LORD will not forsake his people (1 Sam. 12:22). The expositors observe the an inheritance: for the escaped. The expositors observe the them that be escaped: of Benjamin. The expositors observe the a tribe not destroyed: out of Israel. The expositors observe the God preserves his people: the LORD will not forsake his people (1 Sam. 12:22). The verse thus records that the people resolved Benjamin be not destroyed but its inheritance preserved — the care for the integrity of the covenant people, God's providence preserving every tribe, the LORD not forsaking his people for his great name's sake (1 Sam. 12:22).
18:
"Howbeit we may not give them wives of our daughters: for the children of Israel have sworn, saying, Cursed be he that giveth a wife to Benjamin." The witnesses note Israel holding to their oath not to give their daughters to Benjamin. The witnesses note that Gill notes their case was necessitous and desperate, yet they would not give their daughters. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the oath, cursed be he that giveth a wife to Benjamin. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the we may not give — our daughters; the the children of Israel have sworn — the oath; the cursed be he — that giveth a wife; the swear not rashly — swear not at all; let your yea be yea (Matt. 5:34, 37).

The witnesses agree the people held themselves bound by the curse of their oath — "we may not give them wives of our daughters: for the children of Israel have sworn... Cursed be he that giveth a wife to Benjamin": though their brethren's need was desperate, they would not break the letter of their vow; the clinging to the oath's letter, while seeking a way around its purpose, shows the strait conscience of men entangled by rash words; it teaches that a vow unwisely made still binds, and that men so bound are driven to strange shifts, and warns against the snare of swearing what we ought not, "swear not at all... but let your communication be, Yea, yea; Nay, nay" (Matt. 5:34, 37), Israel bound by their oath against Benjamin.

So the witnesses make the verse we may not give: The witnesses note the we may not give: our daughters. The witnesses note the the children of Israel have sworn: the oath. The witnesses note the cursed be he: that giveth a wife. The witnesses note the swear not rashly: swear not at all; let your yea be yea (Matt. 5:34, 37). The expositors observe the we may not give: our daughters. The expositors observe the the children of Israel have sworn: the oath. The expositors observe the cursed be he: that giveth a wife. The expositors observe the swear not rashly: swear not at all; let your yea be yea (Matt. 5:34, 37). The believer is taught to mark the we may not give, the the children of Israel have sworn, the cursed be he, the swear not rashly, and to long for the rule of the King who does all things right. The verse thus records that Israel held to their oath not to give their daughters — clinging to the letter while seeking a way around its purpose, a vow unwisely made still binding, warning against swearing what we ought not, let your communication be yea, yea (Matt. 5:34, 37).
19:
"Then they said, Behold, there is a feast of the LORD in Shiloh yearly in a place which is on the north side of Bethel... and on the south of Lebonah." The witnesses note the elders recalling the yearly feast of the LORD at Shiloh. The witnesses note that Gill notes the elders debated ways to assist Benjamin and preserve the tribe. The witnesses note that there was a feast of the LORD in Shiloh yearly. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a feast of the LORD — at Shiloh; the yearly — the festival; the the place — near Bethel; the keep the feast in truth — with the unleavened bread of sincerity (1 Cor. 5:8).

The witnesses agree the elders fixed on the yearly feast as their occasion — "there is a feast of the LORD in Shiloh yearly": at this festival, when the maidens of Shiloh would come out, they planned for Benjamin to take wives; the using of a holy feast of the LORD as the setting for their device shows the mixture of religion and human contrivance in those days; it teaches that men may bend even sacred occasions to their own shifts, and that the people of God should keep his feasts in purity, not making them the cover of questionable schemes, "keep the feast... with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth" (1 Cor. 5:8), the yearly feast of the LORD at Shiloh.

So the witnesses make the verse a feast of the LORD: The witnesses note the a feast of the LORD: at Shiloh. The witnesses note the yearly: the festival. The witnesses note the the place: near Bethel. The witnesses note the keep the feast in truth: with the unleavened bread of sincerity (1 Cor. 5:8). The expositors observe the a feast of the LORD: at Shiloh. The expositors observe the yearly: the festival. The expositors observe the the place: near Bethel. The expositors observe the keep the feast in truth: with the unleavened bread of sincerity (1 Cor. 5:8). The verse thus records that the elders fixed on the yearly feast of the LORD at Shiloh for their device — the mixture of religion and human contrivance, men bending sacred occasions to their shifts, the people of God to keep his feasts in sincerity and truth (1 Cor. 5:8).
20:
"Therefore they commanded the children of Benjamin, saying, Go and lie in wait in the vineyards;" The witnesses note the elders directing the Benjamites to lie in wait in the vineyards. The witnesses note that Gill notes they ordered the two hundred men that wanted wives, speaking with authority as elders. The witnesses note that they were to lie in wait in the vineyards. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the commanded the children of Benjamin — the two hundred; the go and lie in wait — in the vineyards; the a covert seizure — to evade the oath; the walk uprightly — he that walketh uprightly walketh surely (Prov. 10:9).

The witnesses agree the elders devised a plan of stealth for the two hundred — "Go and lie in wait in the vineyards": the men who lacked wives were to hide and seize the maidens as they came to dance; the contriving of a covert seizure to evade the oath shows how far the people went to escape their self-made bind; it teaches that the entanglements of a rash vow may lead even the leaders of God's people into doubtful and disorderly devices, and that it is better to deal openly and honestly than to compass our ends by stealth, "he that walketh uprightly walketh surely: but he that perverteth his ways shall be known" (Prov. 10:9), the Benjamites bidden to lie in wait.

So the witnesses make the verse lie in wait: The witnesses note the commanded the children of Benjamin: the two hundred. The witnesses note the go and lie in wait: in the vineyards. The verse thus records that the elders directed Benjamin to lie in wait in the vineyards — a covert device to evade the oath, the entanglements of a rash vow leading even leaders into disorderly shifts, better to walk uprightly and surely (Prov. 10:9).
21:
"And see, and, behold, if the daughters of Shiloh come out to dance in dances, then come ye out of the vineyards, and catch you every man his wife of the daughters of Shiloh..." The witnesses note the plan for Benjamin to seize the dancing daughters of Shiloh for wives. The witnesses note that Gill notes the daughters of Shiloh danced by themselves at the festival, so might more easily be taken. The witnesses note that each man was to catch his wife of the daughters of Shiloh. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the daughters of Shiloh — come to dance; the come out of the vineyards — the ambush; the catch every man his wife — the seizure; the decently and in order — let all be done decently and in order (1 Cor. 14:40).

The witnesses agree the maidens' festal dance was made the occasion of their seizure — "if the daughters of Shiloh come out to dance... catch you every man his wife": as the daughters danced in the innocent mirth of the feast, the men were to rush out and carry them off; the turning of a glad festival into the scene of a forced taking shows the disorder of an age without right rule; it teaches how, where the fear of God and lawful order are wanting, even the joys of his feasts are marred by men's devices, and bids us long for the rule that keeps all things in their right and seemly course, "let all things be done decently and in order" (1 Cor. 14:40), the daughters of Shiloh seized at the dance.

So the witnesses make the verse catch every man his wife: The witnesses note the the daughters of Shiloh: come to dance. The witnesses note the come out of the vineyards: the ambush. The witnesses note the catch every man his wife: the seizure. The witnesses note the decently and in order: let all be done decently and in order (1 Cor. 14:40). The expositors observe the the daughters of Shiloh: come to dance. The expositors observe the come out of the vineyards: the ambush. The expositors observe the catch every man his wife: the seizure. The expositors observe the decently and in order: let all be done decently and in order (1 Cor. 14:40). The believer is taught to mark the the daughters of Shiloh, the come out of the vineyards, the catch every man his wife, the decently and in order, and to long for the rule of the King who does all things right. The verse thus records that Benjamin was to seize the dancing daughters of Shiloh for wives — a glad festival turned to a forced taking, the disorder of an age without right rule, bidding us long for order, that all things be done decently and in order (1 Cor. 14:40).
22:
"And it shall be, when their fathers or their brethren come unto us to complain, that we will say unto them, Be favourable unto them for our sakes... ye did not give unto them at this time, that ye should be guilty." The witnesses note the elders' plan to pacify the fathers and to argue the oath was not broken. The witnesses note that Gill notes the fathers might come to complain of the rape of their daughters. The witnesses note that they would say, ye did not give them, that ye should be guilty. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the their fathers complain — to pacify; the be favourable — for our sakes; the ye did not give them — the letter kept; the the weightier matters — omitted the weightier matters of the law (Matt. 23:23).

The witnesses agree the elders prepared a casuistic plea to quiet the fathers — "we did not give unto them... that ye should be guilty": since the men took the maidens by force and the fathers did not give them, they reasoned the oath was kept in the letter; the keeping of the letter while evading the spirit shows the shifts of a conscience seeking to satisfy itself without true uprightness; it teaches the danger of legal evasions that salve the conscience while missing the heart of the matter, and that God looks on the truth within, not the mere form, "ye pay tithe... and have omitted the weightier matters of the law" (Matt. 23:23), the elders' plea to the fathers of Shiloh.

So the witnesses make the verse ye did not give: The witnesses note the their fathers complain: to pacify. The witnesses note the be favourable: for our sakes. The witnesses note the ye did not give them: the letter kept. The witnesses note the the weightier matters: omitted the weightier matters of the law (Matt. 23:23). The expositors observe the their fathers complain: to pacify. The expositors observe the be favourable: for our sakes. The expositors observe the ye did not give them: the letter kept. The expositors observe the the weightier matters: omitted the weightier matters of the law (Matt. 23:23). The believer is taught to mark the their fathers complain, the be favourable, the ye did not give them, the the weightier matters, and to long for the rule of the King who does all things right. The verse thus records that the elders prepared a casuistic plea that the oath was kept in the letter — keeping the letter while evading the spirit, the shifts of a conscience salving itself without uprightness, God looking on truth within, not the form, the weightier matters not to be omitted (Matt. 23:23).
23:
"And the children of Benjamin did so, and took them wives, according to their number, of them that danced, whom they caught: and they... repaired the cities, and dwelt in them." The witnesses note Benjamin taking wives as directed, then repairing their cities and dwelling in them. The witnesses note that Gill notes they laid wait in the vineyards and seized the daughters as they came to dance. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them repairing the cities and dwelling in them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the took them wives — the two hundred; the according to their number — of them that danced; the repaired the cities — restored; the God builds them again — i will build them, and not pull them down (Jer. 24:6).

The witnesses agree the remnant of Benjamin was restored to home and inheritance — "took them wives... repaired the cities, and dwelt in them": the two hundred found wives, and the tribe returned to rebuild its ruined towns and settle again in its land; the restoration of Benjamin shows the mercy that preserved the tribe through all the confusion; it teaches that God in his providence kept Benjamin from perishing, repairing the breach and replanting them, that his purpose for the twelve might stand, "I will build them, and not pull them down" (Jer. 24:6), Benjamin repairing their cities and dwelling in them.

So the witnesses make the verse repaired the cities: The witnesses note the took them wives: the two hundred. The witnesses note the according to their number: of them that danced. The witnesses note the repaired the cities: restored. The witnesses note the God builds them again: I will build them, and not pull them down (Jer. 24:6). The expositors observe the took them wives: the two hundred. The expositors observe the according to their number: of them that danced. The expositors observe the repaired the cities: restored. The expositors observe the God builds them again: I will build them, and not pull them down (Jer. 24:6). The verse thus records that Benjamin took wives and repaired their cities and dwelt in them — the mercy that preserved the tribe through all the confusion, God keeping Benjamin from perishing and replanting them, building them and not pulling them down (Jer. 24:6).
24:
"And the children of Israel departed thence at that time, every man to his tribe and to his family, and they went out from thence every man to his inheritance." The witnesses note Israel departing, every man to his own tribe and inheritance. The witnesses note that Gill notes the war being ended and things settled as well as circumstances would admit. The witnesses note that every man went to his tribe, family, and inheritance. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the departed thence — the assembly; the every man to his tribe — and family; the to his inheritance — homeward; the wars made to cease — he maketh wars to cease (Ps. 46:9).

The witnesses agree the assembly broke up and each returned home — "the children of Israel departed... every man to his tribe and to his family... to his inheritance": with the breach repaired as far as might be, the people dispersed to their own places; the quiet ending of the troubled story shows that even a sorrowful chapter was at last brought to a close; it teaches that after the storms of judgment God grants a measure of rest, and that his people, chastened, return to their dwellings to consider what they have seen, "he maketh wars to cease unto the end of the earth" (Ps. 46:9), the people departing every man to his inheritance.

So the witnesses make the verse every man to his inheritance: The witnesses note the departed thence: the assembly. The witnesses note the every man to his tribe: and family. The witnesses note the to his inheritance: homeward. The witnesses note the wars made to cease: he maketh wars to cease (Ps. 46:9). The expositors observe the departed thence: the assembly. The expositors observe the every man to his tribe: and family. The expositors observe the to his inheritance: homeward. The expositors observe the wars made to cease: he maketh wars to cease (Ps. 46:9). The believer is taught to mark the departed thence, the every man to his tribe, the to his inheritance, the wars made to cease, and to long for the rule of the King who does all things right. The verse thus records that Israel departed, every man to his tribe and inheritance — the troubled story brought at last to a close, God granting rest after the storms of judgment, making wars to cease unto the end of the earth (Ps. 46:9).
25:
"In those days there was no king in Israel: every man did that which was right in his own eyes." The witnesses note the book closing with its great refrain — no king, and every man doing right in his own eyes. The witnesses note that Gill notes there was no supreme magistrate, Joshua being dead and no judge yet risen for these events. The witnesses note that every man did that which was right in his own eyes. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no king in Israel — the refrain; the every man did — what was right; the in his own eyes — the root of disorder; the the King to come — of his government and peace no end (Isa. 9:7).

The witnesses agree the book ends with the refrain that is its key — "In those days there was no king in Israel: every man did that which was right in his own eyes": the whole sad train of idolatry, violence, and confusion in these last chapters sprang from this root, that men owned no rule above their own will; the closing words lay the cause of all the disorder bare, that where God's rule is cast off, every man becomes his own law and all manner of evil follows; it teaches the necessity of a King to govern, the longing that points beyond the earthly throne of David to the true King, Christ, under whose righteous reign alone men are kept from doing what is right in their own eyes, "of the increase of his government and peace there shall be no end" (Isa. 9:7); thus the Book of Judges closes, no king in Israel, and every man doing right in his own eyes.

So the witnesses make the verse no king in Israel: The witnesses note the no king in Israel: the refrain. The witnesses note the every man did: what was right. The witnesses note the in his own eyes: the root of disorder. The witnesses note the the King to come: of his government and peace no end (Isa. 9:7). The expositors observe the no king in Israel: the refrain. The expositors observe the every man did: what was right. The expositors observe the in his own eyes: the root of disorder. The expositors observe the the King to come: of his government and peace no end (Isa. 9:7). The believer is taught to mark the no king in Israel, the every man did, the in his own eyes, the the King to come, and to long for the rule of the King who does all things right. The verse thus records that the book closes with its great refrain — no king in Israel, every man doing right in his own eyes — laying bare that all the disorder sprang from owning no rule above self, teaching the need of a King, pointing beyond David to Christ, of whose government and peace there shall be no end (Isa. 9:7).
